CausalMixGPD: One-arm modeling (unconditional and conditional)

Source: vignettes/02-one-arm.Rmd
02-one-arm.Rmd

This vignette covers “one-arm” usage: you fit one outcome model, then compute posterior summaries and predictions from the fitted distribution. The same interface works for both unconditional models (no covariates) and conditional models (covariate-dependent distributions).

Two design principles matter for interpretation.

First, CausalMixGPD is a density-first workflow: once you have a posterior over the full conditional density, everything else (quantiles, survival, exceedance probabilities, restricted means) is derived coherently from that same fitted distribution.

Second, when GPD = TRUE, the tail beyond the threshold is not an extrapolation of the bulk kernel. It is a dedicated GPD module motivated by peaks-over-threshold theory (Balkema and Haan 1974; Pickands 1975).

What CausalMixGPD returns

A fitted object (mixgpd_fit) represents a posterior over the model parameters, hence a posterior predictive distribution. You typically interact with the fit using:

  • predict(fit, type = "density" | "cdf" | "survival" | "quantile" | "rmean", ...)
  • plot(fit) for diagnostic/summary plots
  • summary(fit) for a compact model summary
  • params(fit) for posterior mean parameters in natural shapes

Internally, posterior draws are stored in fit$samples (and also under fit$mcmc$samples). You rarely need to touch these directly unless you are building custom functionals.

Unconditional model

Data

We use the shipped example nc_pos_tail200_k4, a positive-support dataset with an injected upper tail.

library(CausalMixGPD)
data("nc_pos_tail200_k4")
y <- nc_pos_tail200_k4$y

Model specification and posterior sampling

The code below is the canonical pattern: build a bundle, run MCMC, then call predict() for posterior summaries. (Vignette output is shown from precomputed artifacts so CRAN checks stay deterministic and fast.)

# Build model + priors + MCMC configuration
b <- bundle(
  y = y,
  X = NULL,
  backend = "sb",        # stick-breaking (blocked Gibbs)
  kernel = "lognormal", # positive-support bulk kernel
  GPD = TRUE,             # splice on a GPD tail
  components = 4,
  mcmc = list(niter = 2000, nburnin = 500, thin = 5, nchains = 1, seed = 101)
)

# Run posterior sampling
fit <- mcmc(b)

# Posterior predictive summaries
grid <- seq(min(y), max(y) * 1.5, length.out = 200)
q <- predict(fit, type = "quantile", p = c(0.5, 0.9, 0.95, 0.99), cred.level = 0.90)
d <- predict(fit, y = grid, type = "density", cred.level = 0.90)
S <- predict(fit, y = grid, type = "survival", cred.level = 0.90)

Posterior summaries (precomputed)

Quantiles are often the most interpretable summaries under heavy tails. The table below reports posterior mean quantiles and 90% credible intervals.

Quantile (p) Estimate 90% CrI
0.5 1.619 [1.447, 1.840]
0.9 4.745 [4.170, 5.444]
0.95 6.468 [5.551, 7.670]
0.99 11.686 [9.165, 14.715]

A posterior predictive density overlay (precomputed):

Posterior predictive density for the unconditional fit (precomputed).

Estimation versus prediction

  • Estimation refers to learning the distribution governing the observed sample (posterior over model parameters).
  • Prediction refers to summaries of new outcomes drawn from the posterior predictive distribution.

In CausalMixGPD, predict() is explicitly posterior predictive: it integrates uncertainty in parameters by using the MCMC draws. Credible bands therefore widen when the data are weakly informative in the tails.

Conditional model (regression)

Data and design matrix

We use nc_posX100_p4_k3, which includes covariates in a data frame X.

data("nc_posX100_p4_k3")
y <- nc_posX100_p4_k3$y
Xdf <- nc_posX100_p4_k3$X

# Recommended: include an intercept
X <- model.matrix(~ ., data = Xdf)

Conditional specification (theoretical point of view)

A conditional model means that at least one kernel parameter is allowed to vary with covariates. Conceptually, for a chosen parameter θj(𝐱)\theta_j(\boldsymbol{x}), g{θj(𝐱)}=𝐱⊤𝛃j, g\{\theta_j(\boldsymbol{x})\} = \boldsymbol{x}^\top\boldsymbol{\beta}_j, where gg is a link (identity, exp, etc.). CausalMixGPD exposes this through its param_specs mechanism; “special cases” are just defaults built on this general link idea.

Fit and prediction 

# Fit a conditional model (example settings)
b <- bundle(
  y = y,
  X = X,
  backend = "sb",
  kernel = "lognormal",
  GPD = FALSE,
  components = 4,
  mcmc = list(niter = 2500, nburnin = 600, thin = 5, nchains = 1, seed = 202)
)
fit <- mcmc(b)

# Predict at two covariate profiles (rows of a design matrix)
Xnew <- X[1:2, , drop = FALSE]
ygrid <- seq(quantile(y, 0.01), quantile(y, 0.99), length.out = 160)

q_x <- predict(fit, x = Xnew, type = "quantile", p = c(0.5, 0.9, 0.95), cred.level = 0.90)
d_x <- predict(fit, x = Xnew, y = ygrid, type = "density", cred.level = 0.90)

Conditional summaries (precomputed)

The table below reports posterior mean conditional quantiles for two example covariate profiles (Profile 1 and 2), with 90% credible intervals.

Profile Q(0.5) Q(0.9) Q(0.95)
1 1.484 [1.320, 1.662] 23.314 [11.622, 42.076] 52.351 [21.168, 107.535]
2 1.987 [1.539, 2.479] 31.153 [14.835, 56.453] 69.906 [26.886, 146.645]

A density comparison for the same two profiles (precomputed):

Posterior predictive density for two covariate profiles (precomputed).

Practical notes

Kernel choice and GPD are doing different jobs.

If your goal is accurate tail extrapolation, enable GPD = TRUE (except for kernel = "cauchy", which is intentionally disallowed with GPD). If your goal is a purely nonparametric bulk fit with no parametric tail assumptions, set GPD = FALSE and let the DPM kernel mixture carry everything.

For regression, start simple: keep most parameters “distribution-level” (shared across 𝐱\boldsymbol{x}) and place one parameter in link mode. Expand only after you have checked basic calibration (e.g., predictive quantiles) across covariate strata.

References

Balkema, August A., and Laurens de Haan. 1974. “Residual Life Time at Great Age.” The Annals of Probability 2 (5): 792–804. https://doi.org/10.1214/aop/1176996548.
Pickands, James. 1975. “Statistical Inference Using Extreme Order Statistics.” The Annals of Statistics 3 (1): 119–31. https://doi.org/10.1214/aos/1176343003.
